original_hypothesis.title: OmnichainLogic bridge approvals never expire, enabling delayed execution under changed conditions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bridge approvals in `approvedBridgeTXN` require a minimum waiting time but have no maximum validity window. A previously-approved BridgeRequest can be executed arbitrarily far in the future, potentially after configuration changes, market movement, or changed destination assumptions, increasing risk of unintended asset movement.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While onlyManager-gated (so realism is low under the non-colluding admin model), it’s still a meaningful safety issue: delayed execution may violate operational expectations and incident response procedures (e.g., watchers expect approvals to be short-lived).

proof_of_signal.pattern: approvedBridgeTXN[txn] stores a timestamp and only checks `timestamp + BRIDGE_TXN_WAITING_TIME <= now`; no upper-bound expiry check exists.

preconditions: A bridge transaction hash is approved (approvedBridgeTXN[txn] set)
Execution occurs much later than intended, without re-approval
Operational assumptions expect approvals to be short-lived

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: missing maximum validity window for approval
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No MAX_VALIDITY_PERIOD or nonce-based approval scheme

analysis_questions: Does protocol governance assume approvals are ephemeral and tied to current market state?
Can destinationChainAddress mapping changes make an old BridgeRequest unexpectedly valid/invalid?
Is there an operational mechanism to clear approvals besides toggling transactionHash again?
validation_requirements: Add tests: approve txn, warp time far beyond expected window, ensure still executable (currently yes)
Design review: define intended approval lifetime and implement expiry if needed

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A manager approves a bridge as part of a routine process but execution is delayed. Later, under different conditions, a manager (or compromised key) executes the still-approved bridge without generating a fresh approval event, bypassing intended human oversight timing.
prerequisites: Manager ability to execute startBridgeTransaction at a later time

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential for unintended or unsafe bridging under changed conditions; governance/process risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Add expiry; require re-approval after max age

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests ensuring approvals cannot be executed after a maximum delay

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor age of approvals at execution time

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
